text_clean: This is my third time here and let me tell you there’s been tons of changes. After you get to Aguas Calientes, everyone whether you hired a guide or not must now go to the Ministerio de Culturas and stand in line just to get a number to return later that night or the next day. My understanding from talking to other tour guides is that tour guides used to be able to buy your tickets and they would buy at the Peruvian National price and resell at the international tourist price. To cut down on this corruption now everyone must go and present their passports or their proof of residency (DNI). You are asked how many people in your party and you are given individual numbers for each person. You cannot show up early as they go by the number you were given. Their computer systems are not very stable so expect delays. We were in line a little over 2 hours in the morning and over 3 hours in the evening so almost 6 hours. When your number is called, you go upstairs and have to wait in and move yourself along the winding chairs that lead you back down the stairs to the cashier windows to be asked what circuit you will be getting. There are limited number of tickets per circuit and even though there are “maps” there is nothing showing you what you will see in each circuit and what your pictures will look like. There are screens that can be looked to determine which circuits are still available as you approach the window to place your order. Fee per permit/ticket is $50/person for international traveler regardless of the circuit chosen. Circuit 2A/B is quite popular so for me, I had to go with circuit 3A and that’s the picture I am sharing on this review. Previously on my other 2 visits, I was able to do the whole trip as a day trip and see Machu Picchu and return to Cusco the same day. Now you must stay overnight and some people up to two nights so plan carefully. That said, this area is a lower altitude than Cusco so it is easier to breathe and the town is much more quaint and more enjoyable. That said do not buy your return tickets to Cusco until you have your tour time because you are only allowed to make changes up to 24 hours before and if you try to change at 23 hours and 59 minutes, you will have to buy new tickets. Another piece of advice, when booking bus/train tickets go with Peru Rail instead of Inca Rail as the train cars are much nicer and have wider aisles and better “shows”. With these changes, it almost makes it not worth it to even visit. To me, Machu Picchu is completely overrated and overpriced and inconvenient to get to. Although I’m glad I’ve come 3 times (first time 2021 during covid and raining so pictures were not adequate, second time 2023 with niece and third time 2025 with my wife because she wanted to visit), I’ve thought this every time I’ve come.

To cut down on this corruption now everyone must go and present their passports or their proof of residency (DNI). You are asked how many people in your party and you are given individual numbers for each person. You cannot show up early as they go by the number you were given. Their computer systems are not very stable so expect delays. We were in line a little over 2 hours in the morning and over 3 hours in the evening so almost 6 hours. When your number is called, you go upstairs and have to wait in and move yourself along the winding chairs that lead you back down the stairs to the cashier windows to be asked what circuit you will be getting. There are limited number of tickets per circuit and even though there are “maps” there is nothing showing you what you will see in each circuit and what your pictures will look like. There are screens that can be looked to determine which circuits are still available as you approach the window to place your order. Fee per permit/ticket is $50/person for international traveler regardless of the circuit chosen. Circuit 2A/B is quite popular so for me, I had to go with circuit 3A and that’s the picture I am sharing on this review. Previously on my other 2 visits, I was able to do the whole trip as a day trip and see Machu Picchu and return to Cusco the same day. Now you must stay overnight and some people up to two nights so plan carefully. That said, this area is a lower altitude than Cusco so it is easier to breathe and the town is much more quaint and more enjoyable. That said do not buy your return tickets to Cusco until you have your tour time because you are only allowed to make changes up to 24 hours before and if you try to change at 23 hours and 59 minutes, you will have to buy new tickets. Another piece of advice, when booking bus/train tickets go with Peru Rail instead of Inca Rail as the train cars are much nicer and have wider aisles and better “shows”. With these changes, it almost makes it not worth it to even visit. To me, Machu Picchu is completely overrated and overpriced and inconvenient to get to. Although I’m glad I’ve come 3 times (first time 2021 during covid and raining so pictures were not adequate, second time 2023 with niece and third time 2025 with my wife because she wanted to visit), I’ve thought this every time I’ve come.
label: negativo

text_clean: Not to be missed of you're traveling in Arequipa and even better to make to the trip from Arequipa to Puno (or the other way around) much more interesting. Amazing landscapes going from tropical desert to alti plano, the agricultural terrasses in the value and the deepest canyon in the world. The absolute highlight was the condor viewing point where these beautiful animal fly few meters above your head. We took a two day tour Arequipa - Colca - Puno. Costed 87usd pp.

text_clean: Parque de la.Reserva, es un parque donde su mayor atracción esta en el show de agua, tiene dos estaciones principales y en especial hay un show donde muestran el agua con diferentes colores, formas y además reflejan máscaras o elementos representativos de la región. Es un show muy lindo y me parece muy importante resaltar que en Perú siempre en sus shows, artesanías etc siempre están dando a conocer los elementos representativos de su país. El show dura 15 minutos Valor de la entrada 4 soles, el cual es bastante adsequible Tiene diferentes juegos para los niños Espacios para comer Tiene zona de parqueo Yo fui el viernes y por ser un día festivo, no recomiendo ir ese tipo de días porque es extremadamente lleno, por ende las filas son muy largas, es un poco difícil apreciar el show y es difícil transitar entonces tuvimos que marcharnos después del show y no pudimos seguir viendo con calma las demás cosas que tiene el parque. No hay buena señalización para salir del parque, deberían colocar avisos con luces de neón para ubicarse más fácil, los letreros de salida están muy al piso entonces no se ven.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The Royal Tombs of Sipán Museum near Chiclayo is a must-visit if you love history and archaeology. The pyramid-style architecture itself reflects the ancient Moche culture, and inside you walk through the story of the Lord of Sipán step by step, from excavation to the stunning treasures found in his tomb. The gold, silver, ceramics, and textiles give a deep insight into the artistry and beliefs of the Mochica civilization. The museum is very well maintained — clean, organized, and carefully curated with proper lighting and security that protect these priceless artifacts. The exhibits are presented with respect and detail, making it easy to appreciate the importance of this culture in Peru’s history. Some explanations are only in Spanish and weekends can be a bit crowded, but overall it is one of the best museums in Peru and highly recommended 😊😊😊

text_clean: If you appeared to be in Nasca (like us) and did not want to miss out on #1 tourist attraction here, think twice. To sum up: if you came here specifically to visit the lines, I guess you will like it, go for it. If you’re a casual tourist explorer like us, probably also ok to skip and do something else. Hope it helps to those who have a strong fear of missing out like me! :)

text_clean: Kuélap is absolutely amazing! The place is full of energy and history, surrounded by stunning vegetation and mountain views. The whole tour inside takes about 2 hours, with lots of ruins and impressive walls to explore. Entrance is S/ 20 (online only). To get there you have three options: • Hiking (very hard), • By car (Nogalcucho or Choctamal), • Or the cable car, which we chose and highly recommend! It costs around S/ 27 per person, takes about 25 minutes, and offers breathtaking views. Important note: Kuélap is not only a fortress, but a whole fortified city built by the Chachapoyas culture around 900–1100 AD. The cable car itself was built with French technology and is super modern and safe. Definitely one of the most impressive experiences in northern Peru!

text_clean: Truly wonderful. A must see to check off from the travel list. Plan to stay a night in aguas calientes. Take a private tour guide with you (highly recommended). They can be hired at the time of boarding the bus in aguas calientes. There is room to negotiate the price and should cost you around $50-60 USD, money well spent IMO. Without the tour guide you will just be looking at rock structures with no context to why it was built an how it was used. More tips - 1> There is no direct train to aguas calientes from Cusco. Spend a night in Ollantaytambo and take the train from there. Most hotels there will keep your heavy luggage for a day or 2 if needed for free. 2> Peru Rail has more frequent trips so choose that instead of Inca rail although either one is fine. 3> Do not fall for the Vistadome and Vistadome Observatory class trains marketing. Just take the Expedition class and save some money. The view is just as beautiful. It ok to miss out on the extra 2 inch window size and complimentary snack and fashion show!. I took Expedition and Vistadome (to and from) just to compare and that is my opinion. 4> Choose seats early if possible and take the left side seats for better views of the valley. You are not missing out on anything by taking the right side seats. Left is just slightly better.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: Machu Picchu is, without a doubt, one of the most incredible places I’ve ever visited—possibly the most awe-inspiring place on the planet. Perched high in the Andes Mountains, it feels completely surreal, like you’ve stepped into a dream or a world untouched by time. From the moment you arrive and the clouds part to reveal the ruins, you’re hit with a sense of wonder and reverence that’s hard to put into words. The location is jaw-droppingly beautiful—surrounded by steep, lush peaks, with the Urubamba River winding far below—and the energy of the place is unlike anything else. The architecture and engineering are beyond impressive. The Incas built with such precision and purpose that it’s hard to believe this was all done without modern tools. The stones fit together so tightly that not even a blade of grass can slide between them. Every building, terrace, and path has meaning—whether it’s astronomical, agricultural, or spiritual. The Temple of the Sun, the Intihuatana Stone, the perfectly aligned solstice windows—it all shows how advanced and in tune with nature this civilization really was. What truly elevates Machu Picchu from a historical site to a life-changing destination is the feeling it gives you. There’s a quiet, sacred atmosphere here. Even with other tourists around, there are moments of total silence where it feels like the mountains are speaking. I found myself stopping often—not just to admire the view, but to take it all in on a deeper level. Sitting near the edge, watching mist drift across the stones, listening to the wind, you realize this is more than a ruin—it’s a reminder of how small we are and how powerful human creativity and connection to the Earth can be. If you’re able, I highly recommend hiking up Huayna Picchu or Machu Picchu Mountain. The trails are challenging, but the views from the top are absolutely unforgettable. You’ll see the ruins from above, laid out like a cosmic map between the clouds and peaks. It’s breathtaking. Whether you get there by train or take the multi-day trek along the Inca Trail, just getting to Machu Picchu is an adventure—and totally worth it. The entire experience is unforgettable from start to finish. This is not just a place to see—it’s a place to feel. It stays with you, long after you’ve left. If you’re thinking of going, stop thinking and just go. Machu Picchu isn’t just a destination. It’s a soul-stirring, humbling, and deeply moving experience that will leave you changed.

The architecture and engineering are beyond impressive. The Incas built with such precision and purpose that it’s hard to believe this was all done without modern tools. The stones fit together so tightly that not even a blade of grass can slide between them. Every building, terrace, and path has meaning—whether it’s astronomical, agricultural, or spiritual. The Temple of the Sun, the Intihuatana Stone, the perfectly aligned solstice windows—it all shows how advanced and in tune with nature this civilization really was. What truly elevates Machu Picchu from a historical site to a life-changing destination is the feeling it gives you. There’s a quiet, sacred atmosphere here. Even with other tourists around, there are moments of total silence where it feels like the mountains are speaking. I found myself stopping often—not just to admire the view, but to take it all in on a deeper level. Sitting near the edge, watching mist drift across the stones, listening to the wind, you realize this is more than a ruin—it’s a reminder of how small we are and how powerful human creativity and connection to the Earth can be. If you’re able, I highly recommend hiking up Huayna Picchu or Machu Picchu Mountain. The trails are challenging, but the views from the top are absolutely unforgettable. You’ll see the ruins from above, laid out like a cosmic map between the clouds and peaks. It’s breathtaking. Whether you get there by train or take the multi-day trek along the Inca Trail, just getting to Machu Picchu is an adventure—and totally worth it. The entire experience is unforgettable from start to finish. This is not just a place to see—it’s a place to feel. It stays with you, long after you’ve left. If you’re thinking of going, stop thinking and just go. Machu Picchu isn’t just a destination. It’s a soul-stirring, humbling, and deeply moving experience that will leave you changed.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: The mysterious Nazca lines are an unusual sight that will leave visitors with a sense of awe. These huge images on the desert floor were relatively undiscovered until planes flying over the area in the 1920s saw the lines from the air and realized they formed distinct patterns and images. Until that time there was some recognition of the hillside drawings near Nazca and Paracas, which can be seen from ground level. However, the huge drawings on the flat desert floor are so large that it requires an aerial view to be appreciated. From the air, it is possible to see 70 different plant and animal drawings as well as hundreds of lines and other geometrical shapes. Some of these lines stretch as long as 10 kilometers, and they are spread over hundreds of square kilometers. Most notable among the figures are a lizard measuring 180 meters long, a condor with a 130-meter wingspan, and several others that include a monkey, hummingbird, killer whale, and spider. Although it is not known exactly who created the lines or how and why, theories hold that the lines were the product of the Paracas and Nazca cultures sometime between 900 BC and AD 600. Why they were created is the subject of much debate. Some of the theories put forward suggest the lines were a type of astronomical calendar for agriculture, an alien landing pad, a running track, walkways joining ceremonial sites, or part of a water cult. The lines were created by removing the dark surface layer of stones and piling them at the sides of the lines, creating a contrast between the dark stones and the exposed lighter soil below. Flights can be booked in advance or on a walk-in, first-come first-serve basis. Approximately four kilometers outside of Nazca are the Cantalloc Aqueducts. Built around AD 300 to 600, the aqueducts were designed to provide a year-round water source for the area. They conduct water from the mountain springs down to Nazca by means of underground canals. Some of the Cantalloc Aqueducts are still used by farmers in the area. Also of interest in the area is the Cemetery of Chauchilla, which contains Nazca remains and mummies

text_clean: These mountaintop ruins are breathtaking, and the stone construction of the houses, temples, and walls are superb. The only problem is that half the site, including the iconic front walls and gateway, are currently under construction and inaccessible. There is a cable car recently built from the town of Nuevo Tingo to Kuelap, but it was not working the day I went, so we had to take a private vehicle and then hike up the very top of the mountain to get there. But once things are up and running again, getting to Kuelap should be much easier, and the site is well worth a visit.

text_clean: Lo que menos nos gustó de nuestro viaje a Perú. Las líneas en sí son interesantes, pero todo lo que rodea a la gestión deja mucho que desear. Es muy caro para lo que ves, más el pago de 2 tasas diferentes una vez allí. Además, el vuelo en la avioneta dura media hora y las explicaciones son prácticamente inexistentes más allá de "aquí tenemos la figura del mono" En esa línea. Si pillara más de paso... Bueno. Pero pilla muy a desmano para solo media hora de sobrevuelo y ninguna explicación. text_clean: "If you're looking for a unique and unforgettable experience in Peru, I highly recommend visiting the Nasca Plateau. The expansive desert landscape is truly awe-inspiring, and the ancient lines etched into the ground will leave you in wonder. It's a testament to the ingenuity and skill of the Nasca people who created them so long ago. The best way to fully appreciate the magnitude and intricacy of the lines is from above, with a scenic flight. The pilots are knowledgeable and provide fascinating insights into the history, culture, and mystery surrounding the lines. Overall, the Nasca Plateau is a must-visit destination for anyone interested in ancient civilizations and mysteries of the world."

text_clean: The guards act irresponsibly and put the guests' health at risk by taking away their water. Attached is a photo of the entrance where this happend. We did Circuit 3 and got through the entrance of circuit 3/4 without any problems. When we entered again (through the entrance to the Mountain, which is also for Circuit 1+2) our backpack was suddenly too big. We had (for 2 people) 3l of water in a drinking system built into the backpack, 2 breads in a foldable can, sunscreen and 2 rain jackets. So nothing big, no plastic waste and our backpack fit in the small model box. We discussed in Spanish whether they really wanted to take away our water for the strenuous hike up the mountain in the midday heat, but they remained adamant. This irresponsible behavior inevitably leads to dehydration, which is dangerous since there is no way to buy more water. Otherwise the purchased ticket will be useless. I am horrified by the conditions there and how carelessly they provoke medical emergencies.

text_clean: With all of the beauty of Machu Pichu you need to understand that taking the first 1-3 busses at Dawn is critical to your day. My first day I made the mistake of hiking at 6:30 am. Big mistake! When I arrived I thought I was at Disneyland on a holiday weekend. Many people that can’t walk, perhaps first time in a pair of hiking boots.. the crowds were incredible. As day 1 wore on, it looked like an Instagram/ Facebook selfy-fest!!! Day two, I rose at 4 am and caught the second bus. Two different experiences! Do yourself a favor and get there early!! Sorry to sound bitter, but the number of tickets should be cut by 50% Take a peek at photo #10, my buddy took to understand what 5:30 am lineup looks like 😳

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. text_clean: The site itself is exactly what you see in the photos. The views around are nice. It's very crowded though, and buying tickets is a bureaucratic nightmare: You have to queue for a number that allows you to buy a ticket at a certain time. Then have to come back at that time, listen for your number (which is called out by some guy in Spanish to a crowd of hundreds), then queue again. Then you have to pick one of 6 routes and 3 additions, most of which will no longer be available in the upcoming days, depending on your luck. The routes that are available usually don't allow entrance to the actual site since those are booked way in advance via a terrible online system. If you want to go at the start of the year, you cannot book tickets in advance, because you can only by tickets for the current year. Also, this online system is down or unresponsive for most of January. Even though we booked through an agency 6 months in advance, they were not able to secure our tickets so we had to queue. In the end, our group were not able to actually visit the site, only take a photo from top.... There are so many nice things to see in Peru and South America... Including much larger and more impressive Inca settlements, which are much cheaper or even free to visit... It's really a shame the Peruvian makes it so difficult to visit this place. Unless you really really want to visit Machu Picchu, I would recommend spending your time elsewhere.

Unless you really really want to visit Machu Picchu, I would recommend spending your time elsewhere.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: This is a cool historical site surrounded by sea of police corruption. We were there in December and five (5) police shakedowns later, my son and I made it from the Nazca desert back to Lima. Our strategies included dividing up the $100 dollars the Hilton gave us on credit (after the police stole all of our money a day earlier). We divided the $ and hid it in various locations in the car. Each time I would reload my wallet with only $20 dollars. Each time the Peru Police pulled us over and demanded money for not having a fire extinguisher in the car, for having the lights on, for NOT having the lights on...they just pulled out their little book of violations and pointed to a violation and demanded payment on the spot. Each time my wallet only had $20 and we said that was all we had and we would be happy to pay by credit card (which of course they could not take...). We kept our original passports hidden and just used the copies of our documents that the Hilton made for us. We would say that "the police already kept our original documents and we don't have them anymore and are going to the American Consulate in Lima to ask for them to be replaced." One group of police actually let us go with no payment at all...so that was positive.... I've been to 84 countries. This is the most corrupt place that I have ever seen. Sometimes the traffic fines come with death threats. Seeing the lines by aircraft was interesting but not worth the price of all the police terrorism to get down there from Lima.

text_clean: Recientemente visité la Plaza Mayor de Lima y me pareció muy pequeña en comparación con otras plazas principales de ciudades similares. Aunque es un lugar histórico y emblemático, me esperaba algo más grande y majestuoso. Sin embargo, es un lugar lleno de vida y energía, con hermosos edificios y una gran cantidad de restaurantes y tiendas. Creo que es un lugar que vale la pena visitar, pero no esperen una plaza grandiosa.

text_clean: Creo que no hay relación en el costo y el tiempo que te lleva llegar al Machu Picchu, con lo que es. Obvio que es hermoso! Y hay que conocerlo, pero hay muchísima gente, compré el circuito 3 por el waina Picchu, una guia nos cobro 50 soles , que si es necesaria la explicación, pero este circuito son 200 mts? ...luego subis al waina que es espectacular, cansador y no apto para los que tienen miedo a las alturas, ahí es libre el tiempo, pero después cuando bajas , seguís por tu circuito hacia la salida, pero sin explicación de guía y toda la gente saliendo hace que te vayas rápido. El pueblo de aguas calientes me pareció muy lindo!!! Muchos bares, eso sí, todo en inglés, está bien que haya turistas, pero una carta en el resto en castellano!!! Luego camine 10 km, lindo...pero obviamente volví en tren ( 70 dólares) no iba a arriesgar mi vida , menos que siempre llueve.... Conclusión es caro? Si...hay que conocerlo? Si...peroooo hay otros lugares que valen la pena, esto es puro comercio.

text_clean: A very good place to visit in the north of Perú. It is one of the most fascinating archaeology sites in all of the country, and definitely up there in the world as well. Unbelievably hidden from the outside world for many hundreds of years until a local pastor came across it under the brush and now have it displayed for the world to enjoy. They say the city is not in a fortress, so perhaps it is best described as a walled city, but one could imagine what that might look like. It is deceivingly small from the outside as you have to walk around it to get in, then once you’re inside, you realise how large it actually is. Admission is s/25, or s/15 for students like myself. The site can be accessed by driving or for those that do not mind heights so much, the cable car (telecabina). Would highly recommend to anyone interesting in the Chachapoyas people and history.

text_clean: I came to Arequipa to enjoy the city and to visit the Monastery was something I was excited about doing. I arrived yesterday and I was extremely disappointed to find that there was no way for someone like me to enter and enjoy this beautiful piece of architecture and history.. I have muscular dystrophy and to use stairs is basically impossible for me. I understand that it is a historic city and things sometimes can’t be changed for preservation purposes - but to have no ramp or mini platform elevator or alternate entrance or even a handrail for the stairs was shocking and disappointing to say the least. I only write this review to let others know who may be physically altered (senior citizens or pregnant women) or handicapped like me that this will be what you find when you arrive there. It makes no sense for a place that is so historical and beautiful and important, a tourist treasure of the city, to not be accesible to everyone. I was still able to enjoy some of the beautiful architecture from the outside, but it was very sad not to be able to experience this.

text_clean: Tuve la oportunidad de visitar el lugar en 2018 y, en ese momento, fue una experiencia única. Pude hacer el recorrido saliendo de Cusco y regresando el mismo día. Lamentablemente, hoy el costo de ingreso se ha incrementado de manera considerable y, con el nuevo sistema de circuitos, ya no es posible recorrer todo el sitio. Prácticamente es obligatorio quedarse al menos una noche en Machu Picchu Pueblo para subir al día siguiente, lo que hace muy difícil realizar todo el recorrido en una sola jornada. El lugar sigue siendo increíble, pero la experiencia ya no se siente igual.

text_clean: As a tourist you really do not need to join a tour to visit the gardens. I took an Uber from Miraflores which was c.20s, paid the 5s entrance fee and additional 5s entrance for the k pop area and finally a c.20s Uber back to Miraflores. So I spent 50s in total vs tours which are 3-4x the price. As per other reviews, some fountains are shut for maintenance and all the fountains have metal barriers around; so it’s not the best atmosphere. The light show at 7.15 was good!

text_clean: Excelente lugar para visitar en tu paso por Chachapoyas. Hay sol, pero es agradable, suele llover, recomendable ir con ropa impermeable. Para llegar a la fortaleza el camina está señalizado, es en subida unos 300 metros z luego ya es plano. Llegas a la fortaleza y te deja maravillado, tienes una vista impresionante. Te conectas con la naturaleza. Les recomiendo la agencia Kuelap Tours. Ahh, el trato es muy agradable. Me olvidaba de comentarles que antes de subir a la fortaleza hay tiendas para comprar recuerdos, tienen que hacerlo.

text_clean: It was one of the highlights of our 2 weeks tour of Peru. My recommendation is to get there early, as the mid morning sun cast the best light on the white washed sillar walls and the red and indigo interior. Do pay for a guide to explain the rich history behind the convent. The guided tour is done by the official onsite guide and in various languages and it only cost Sol 5 per person and you pay directly to the guide at the end of the guided tour. The guide we had was a female guide by the name of Alexandria and she is very knowledgeable and speaks perfect English. It is so fascinating to know that the convent was somewhat like the “Elite” convent only the rich and famous can afford to send their daughters. Many of the nuns had several (some up to 4-5) maids with them the entire time they were in the monastery! The tour will take about an hour to conclude, after which, spend another hour at least to wonder around the labyrinth-like convent and explore many of the living quarters of the nun. If you have time, maybe a mid-morning coffee in the picturesque cafe. Photo taking is permitted. All in all, plan to spend at least 2-2.5 hours here. It is very much well worth the time!

text_clean: Educate yourselves well on what you want to do on the mountain and where you want to spend your time. (Machu Picchu (4 hours), Huayna Picchu (3 hours) + caves (1hour?), Inca bridge (1 hour), Citadel (1 hour), Sun Gate (2 hours), etc). Our guide wasted almost two hours of our entry time which really limited what our options were for our personal goals in our tired condition. My partner and I did a 4 day/3 night Jungle adventure which involved quite a lot of hiking. By the time we got to Machu Picchu our feet and legs were pretty destroyed. text_clean: Pretty nice and interesting historical place, if a little overhyped. Our experience was dampened by a terrible guide who repeated the same 3-4 facts that could be found in any guidebook over the course of 45mins. Afterwards we went round again and there was enough information available on the walls and the leaflet you get given with entry (unlike in several other attractions in Peru) to enjoy a slower self guided tour. That being said, we heard a couple of more diligent guides while on our second lap so perhaps we were just unlucky.

text_clean: El lugar es fascinante y la visita guiada fue muy buena. Le daría cinco estrellas por el valor del sitio, la calidad de la guía y el cuidado del jardín de plantas tradicionales; sin embargo, los cuys y las llamas que tienen están en muy mal estado. En particular, los cuys están hacinados en grandes cantidades en espacios pequeños. Están sobre sus propias heces. En el poco tiempo que estuve ahí vi como uno atacaba a otros como resultado de estar tan apretados. No veo la necesidad de tener a estos animalitos en ese espacio tan reducido.

text_clean: Ayer fui al circuito mágico del agua. Una pésima idea, fui con un adulto mayor y niños, no hay asientos disponibles y en una calor extrema, no hay donde taparse o asombrarse del del sol cuando quisimos sentarnos en el en la zonas verdes nos dijeron que estaba prohibido y tuvimos que estar parados cerca de las tres horas que estuvimos con el adulto mayor y no pudimos sentarnos a ningún lugar ya que todo estaba repleto; pero tengo que hacer una observación. Hay muchos juegos para niños que están posicionados en muchas áreas verdes,mientras a la gente que paga una entrada, no se nos permitía ni siquiera sentarnos dentro de estas zonas ya que no hay asientos suficiente, me parece muy pésima idea es un sitio donde uno quiere ir a a observar las aguas, las piletas, etc. pero lo han convertido en un sitio donde se está monetizando y muchas personas que venimos de fuera quedamos decepcionados , Prefiero ir a otros lugares donde te traten mejor. No les sugiero que vayan, tengan otras opciones para poder salir un fin de semana a divertirse acá en Lima.

text_clean: Mis recomendaciones en general: 1. Solo quédense 1 noche a lo mucho en aguas calientes , no hay nada que hacer y todo es carísimo, si pueden hacer el pasadía mejor. 2. Pueden ahorrar se el dinero del bus si caminan a machupichu, es muy lindo y con tiempo suben en unas 1 hora y media y la verdad es lo mejor de ir a machupichu. La dificultad no es alta pese a unos escalones. 3. Compren circuito 2. 4. Los guías son súper groseros sino les compras su tour, somos colombianos y cuando le dijimos a una guía que no queríamos el servicio nos dijo pobres. 5. Es linda la vista pero la logística es muy mala, la gente de los tours se atiborra 6. Lleven gorra, gafas, filtro solar. 7. Compren los tiquetes del ciurcuito un día antes antes de la entrada a machupichu, comprarlo meses antes por la página no es necesario, siempre hay tiquetes (si vas a hacer esto quédate un día antes porque solo así los venden)

Pero le ofrece al turista que si quiere si paga un monto de 10 soles extra ella hace el recorrido inmediatamente. Escuchando las conversaciones con los demás turistas reconocimos esto como una estafa. Pues Milagros y los demás trabajadores ofrecían esta "oportunidades" forma diferente. No es costoso pero al sentirnos estafados es muy reprochable. Esperamos se tomen medidas y se investigue el caso, no es bueno para el turista sentirse estafado.

text_clean: Most of these mysterious geoglyphs were created by the Nazca people, who lived in the area from 200 to 700 CE. But the researchers believe that some of the newfound ones were created even earlier - by the Paracas and Topará people, who lived there around 500 BCE to 200 CE. The lines have been a source of fascination and wonder for decades. Created by pushing aside the top layer of red desert pebbles to reveal the pale layer underneath, these geoglyphs span vast tracts of the Nazca Desert, between the towns of Nazca and Palpa. Some of them form geometric shapes, some are simple lines, and some are combined into elaborate depictions of animals and objects. The most wondrous thing about these ancient symbols is that you often can't see what they depict from ground level. You have to get up in the air to discern the patterns, which is why their full glory wasn't understood until after the invention of airplanes.

text_clean: Stunning place - but for the life of us we could not work out what the 70 soles per person fee was going to. The whole thing stank of corruption and lining local officials pockets - we were told it was for tourism infrastructure, but this was few and far between. And as a tourist I'm already paying for my hotel, transport and food that is going to benefit the local economy, I'm not completely sure what extra this was going to - just a payment to look at nature? Yes, the roads were good, but I'm pretty sure that's more because of the plethora of mining companies in the area. Seemed just like seeing foreign tourists as open wallets and was definitely not a great side to see of Peru.

Another things whihc isnt easy to find beforehand is that if you are above 100kg most likely they will charge you extra as they will have to reduce the number of passengers on the plane.

text_clean: The Nazca lines were amazing. I definitely recommend seeing them. I am generally scared of flying but enjoyed the flight for the first 5-8 minutes. Unfortunately I got motion sickness and spent the rest of the flight with my eyes closed. Not sure if pills help at all because a girl on the flight also got sick despite taking them beforehand. Another things whihc isnt easy to find beforehand is that if you are above 100kg most likely they will charge you extra as they will have to reduce the number of passengers on the plane.

text_clean: Lo ideal es quedarse una o dos noches en alguno de los pueblos cercanos, como Yanque o Chivay, y disfrutar de las profundidades de este paisaje con un coche de alquiler. Hay que ir parando continuamente para hacer fotos o para descubrir a lo lejos algún pueblecito aislado al otro lado del río o algún huerto salpicado de los colores vivos de la ropa de los lugareños. El mirador del cóndor es un hervidero de turistas, no sé cómo estas aves siguen sobrevolando la zona con tanta tranquilidad y no buscan otros pagos.

text_clean: The grand dame of Lima’s history - Plaza Mayor de Lima, where colonial guilt meets postcard charm. Founded in 1535 by Francisco Pizarro (whose statue was exiled, then sneakily reinstated), this place has seen it all.. declarations of independence, public executions, and more balcony drama than a telenovela. Surrounded by impressive facades like the Cathedral, Presidential Palace, and City Hall, it’s like standing in the middle of a well-behaved architectural argument. The bronze fountain in the center has outlived more presidents than Peru can count, and once even served pisco to the masses, viva la fuente! Come for the history, stay for the perfectly photogenic symmetry. But don’t expect solitude, the only thing louder than the tour groups are the pigeons claiming turf like feathered conquistadors. Still, whether you’re admiring colonial craftsmanship or plotting escape from the sun, Plaza Mayor delivers drama, dignity and a dash of Spanish déjà vu.

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.

text_clean: For me, it's an Overrated marketing success of an abandoned settlement. I will submit that the majestic mountains and valleys are the main draw and attraction. The hyped settlement architecture? It is just a cluster of abandoned stone/rock/mud low 'buildings' meant for 3-400 families. Sure, some walls are tightly stacked with big chipped/carved boulders/rocks and even a blade can't get thru the gaps... The mountain terrace carved/landscaped for agriculture is not unique to the Inca. The use of gravity to channel water is common amongst other old civilisations too... With a good guide, the story telling of Inca civilisation and the Inca connection to mother earth and nature can be engaging. Know too that getting there to MP is a long journey and time wasting pain, compounded with an eventual convoluted Q system to get expensive tickets. (It can be a 2 day Q for a 3hrs just one section visit of MP). .. And a tourist trap village venue to take a bus up to MP. When there, Also beware the nasty midges/sand flies which can carry the deadly oropouch virus. Only this is free. 🤣😂 Oh, the story of the hacked down obelisk to accommodate the Prez helicopter landing is outlandish.

text_clean: Not handicapped friendly. I would love to tell you about this place but I opted not to tour. Reason was that all tours are guided. Handicapped people can only do 1/2 of the tour. The tour then leaves the handicapped person at a particular place in the very hot peruvian sun for however long the able bodied people take to finish the rest of the tour. We were told 30-45 minutes. Can you imagine putting your 85 year old grandmother old in the sun in the middle of the day for that long? I cannot and cannot relate to what the Peruvians are thinking!!! Obviously they are not. I consider that cruel and that I why i cannot comment except to say your handicapped policies suck!!!

text_clean: Amazing ruins of the ancient holy city of Incas. The place doesn't need any explanation, it's among 7 world wonders and very well known. However I believe the local authorities did exploited this place too much without caring too much about the experience of the visit. Although the daily capacity of visitors is capped this place looks way to busy and filled with people who are just there to make selfies. My suggestion is to reach it via Inca trail, even just the last piece of the trekking, so that you can see it from the top of Porta del Sol. Lastly the entry costs have crazly skyrocket in last 10 years with little if no sense.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Esta localizado en Lambayeque Perú. Su diseño arquitectónico tiene similitud con las Piramides preincaicas de la cultura Mochica. El objetivo del museo es exhibir la tumba del señor de Sipan que fue descubierta en 1987 por los arqueólogos peruanos. Eate museo es de tres pisos, desde el segundo piso se observa la tumba exacta de una cámara funebre del antiguo dignatario. En el centro del museo se puede apreciar la sala Real Mochica donde se encuentra el Señor de Sipan con su rropa de guerrero y pectoral de oro además de otras joyas.

La caminata dura aproximadamente 2 horas y es muy difícil para niños y adultos mayores, incluso personas que no tienen mucha resistencia física.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. When visiting one of the wonders of the world it seems insane that zero information is provided to let people learn more about the sites rich past. I presume this is to encourage the use of guides, but regardless a small number of boards, even just indicating what certain spaces are or what they were used for, should be a minimum. - Toilets should be free. destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Hoy descubrí la historia del señor de sipán El museo un amplio complejo muy agradable con una zona trasera con casetas de artesanos La entrada se paga al entrar al recinto, para meter el coche son dos soles y la entrada de adultos son 10 soles No se puede acceder ni con móviles, cámaras ni bolsos al museo. Las taquillas las tienen en el exterior( cosa poco usual y de primeras un poco desconcertante, en fin) Puedes pagar por un guia 30soles. El museo se divide en tres plantas, no es muy grande se ve en una hora aproximadamente. Lo peor que vi es la oscuridad que tiene el museo. Las vitrinas con texto no se puede leer bien.

text_clean: Sugiero hacer reserva al número que está en su página. No pude apreciar la vista de la huaca, mi mesa estaba muy lejos y al parecer dan preferencia a grupos para ubicarlos cerca. La comida estuvo muy buena, ordenamos un lomo saltado y un tacu tacu con sábana de lomo. Y de postre, esfera de café y caramelo. Los tragos también estuvieron en su punto. No les recomiendo ir en parejas si desean algo más romántico, creo que hay opciones mejores; pero, para reunirse en grupo y celebrar algo especial sí lo valdría.

text_clean: Para ir al machu picchu tienes que comprar las entradas con 2 o 3 meses de antelacion. He ido con mi familia y no es lo que esperaba, tienes que pagar por todo, si vas a subir a las montañas tienes que ir preparado con mucha agua y ropa comoda. Además para entrar al machu picchu tienes que pagar por circuitos, antes no hacia falta ya que con el circuito 1 se podia ver todo, ahora tienes que pagar por ver cada cosa, por ejemplo: en el circuito 1 solo ves el machu picchu desde fuera y subir la montaña, nada más. Si subes las montañas es bajo tu responsabilidad y si te caes o te haces daño tienes que pagar mucho dinero para un medico (creo que mas de 100 dolares). Tampoco es que al subir la montaña haiga una recompensa tremenda, subi la montaña del circuito 1 y solo habian unas sillas y un letrero de cuanta altura tiene la montaña. Puedes sacar buenas fotos pero nada más, si no traes agua, mejor ni lo intentes, tienes tiempo limite hasta las 12:00 mediodia para subir y se tarda unas 4 horas a paso lento. Si vas a subir tambien recomiendo llevar repelentes para los mosquitos ya que hay muchos mosquitos. Si te gusta escalar este viaje es para ti, pero si eres un poco sedentario no vale la pena pagar tanto solo para subir una montaña y tomarte unas cuantas fotos, por cierto, si llueve olvidate de subir los cerros, es imposible y probablemente te rompas o te hagas daño o peor. No hay seguridad alguna. Sinceramente es bonito para pasar el dia pero tienes que comprar los demas circuitos (1,2,3,4) , porque si no, no puedes ver nada. Recalco otra vez, tienes que pagar por todo, TODO. Las fotos son preciosas pero no todo lo que brilla es oro. Se podria distribuir mejor y que todo no sea tan caro. Igualmente muy bonito Perú y hermoso el machu picchu aunque con lo que cobran se podria mejor muchisimo.

text_clean: I recently visited Machu Picchu with my partner, and while the site itself is undeniably breathtaking, I am deeply concerned about the lack of proper emergency response systems in place for visitors experiencing medical emergencies. During our visit, my partner, as he got out of the bus, slipped because of the uneven surface and slippery as the road was wet, he injured his ankle. He suffered a concerning medical episode— things unfolded very quickly - he was in a lot of pain, he collapsed and experienced convulsions - due to what we believe was a combination of altitude, and intense pain of the injury. In such a high-traffic and physically demanding site, we expected a prompt and trained medical response. Unfortunately, that was not the case. There was no visible or accessible medical staff nearby, and it took far too long to get any form of assistance. The staff present seemed untrained to handle emergencies such as this and there was no coordinated protocol to help us manage the situation. Thankfully my partner recovered but that was a very stressful moment for us. I am writing this not in anger, but in hopes that the management will: Review and strengthen their emergency protocols. Ensure that trained medical staff are stationed on-site in different posts at all times Install clearly marked emergency points or communication stations Train staff in basic first aid and response procedures Machu Picchu is one of the most visited tourist destinations in the world. With the number of visitors—including elderly people, children, and those not acclimated to the altitude—accidents and health emergencies are not rare, and a more robust system must be put in place to prevent avoidable harm. I truly hope the management takes this seriously. The experience itself is magical, but basic safety and human response must be part of that experience.

text_clean: Pasé una muy mala experiencia en la zona de ingreso a la maravilla. Soy una mujer indígena kichwa Otavaleña (Ecuador) y llegué usando mi ropa tradicional (nada extravagante ni fuera de lugar). La srta de ingreso me impedía entrar indicando que no podía ingresar vestida así, a pesar de indicarle lo anterior. Se portó antipática, nada amable. Me dijo que use una chompa para cubrir mi ropa (no llevé chompa ni nada más). También dijo algo que yo iba a hacer videos de publicidad y cosas parecidas... le dije que no era así y que si deseaba podía revisar mi celular tanto al ingreso como a la salida. Simplemente no quisieron escuchar. Luego me mandaron a retirarme de la entrada con un guardia (Sr Guaman). Al cabo de nada, empecé a sufrir una crisis acompañada de taquicardia, llamaron a primeros auxilios, quienes me acompañaron hasta estabilizarme y luego me dejaron entrar. Fue una experiencia terrible. Necesitan más capacitación y empatia, les recuerdo que el mundo está lleno de diversidad cultural, que antes éramos el Tahuantinsuyo y que compartíamos y seguimos compartiendo creencias, tradiciones, lenguas y mucho más!

Al cabo de nada, empecé a sufrir una crisis acompañada de taquicardia, llamaron a primeros auxilios, quienes me acompañaron hasta estabilizarme y luego me dejaron entrar. Fue una experiencia terrible. Necesitan más capacitación y empatia, les recuerdo que el mundo está lleno de diversidad cultural, que antes éramos el Tahuantinsuyo y que compartíamos y seguimos compartiendo creencias, tradiciones, lenguas y mucho más!
label: negativo

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The content of the museum is amazing and some of the best I've seen in South America. Unfortunately the museum is massively let down by the lack of story and content conveyed by the information boards. Also none of the information on the second and third, the lowest level, has any translation into other languages and the top level only into English (about 25% of the whole museum) so its pointless going if you want to learn and not just see pretty objects. I believe they have the option of a guide to squeeze more money from you if you like. I'd recommend reading up beforehand so you know what your going to see.

text_clean: El sitio es increíble, le doy un 2 por la penosa organización que hay y el dinero que te dejas para poder verlo, un completo sin sentido. El primer punto negativo es tener que elegir que parte del lugar ver me parece un sinsentido, nosotros pagábamos por el tour 3A con Huaynapichu que no es especialmente económico y nos fuimos con la sensación de que nos faltaba por ver una gran parte. Además de que tienes el tiempo cobrado, en nuestro caso solo podíamos estar dentro 2h. Hay que añadir que antes de poder entrar hay que esperar una hora en el pueblo para que te suba el autobús, y después otros cuarenta minutos para entrar. Nuestra entrada era a las 7.00, terminamos entrando a las 7.30. El tour y la visita a la montaña fue bien, pero al salir estuvimos otra hora esperando hasta montar en el autobús de vuelta al pueblo. No me quiero imaginar estas esperas con frío o lluvia, pues estás completamente a la intemperie. Es algo que la autoridad competente tiene que pensar, no pueden tener a la gente en estas condiciones. En la vida habíamos visto algo así.

text_clean: The place itself is certainly unique and very special. The organization and the associated bureaucracy is a disgrace, also because 1.7 million people visit this site every year, the organization should be improved. At first, our train didn't run and we had to wait in a larger group with two small children in undignified conditions in front of a gate with hundreds of others. We spent a total of over 1600 dollars on the train tickets alone, so you would at least expect decent conditions. After a delay of around 2.5 hours, we boarded the train. The information policy at the station was practically non-existent. On the day of the tour (after all the necessary documents had been submitted), our tickets were checked a total of 5 times (!!!) and we were asked for all our IDs 4 times (in a group of 10 people). At the entrance to the Puchho Piccho we had to wait again for about 15 minutes (in the rain), there was again bureaucratic dung, if necessary it would have been helpful if the person had not been alone. If you now think about who is enriching themselves in this place and on the way there, the whole thing is outrageous for the Peruvian people. A few rich people are getting much richer here and the poor remain poor. But it is inconceivable that the organization and processes associated with the visit are not being improved.

At first, our train didn't run and we had to wait in a larger group with two small children in undignified conditions in front of a gate with hundreds of others. We spent a total of over 1600 dollars on the train tickets alone, so you would at least expect decent conditions. After a delay of around 2.5 hours, we boarded the train. The information policy at the station was practically non-existent. On the day of the tour (after all the necessary documents had been submitted), our tickets were checked a total of 5 times (!!!) and we were asked for all our IDs 4 times (in a group of 10 people). At the entrance to the Puchho Piccho we had to wait again for about 15 minutes (in the rain), there was again bureaucratic dung, if necessary it would have been helpful if the person had not been alone. If you now think about who is enriching themselves in this place and on the way there, the whole thing is outrageous for the Peruvian people. A few rich people are getting much richer here and the poor remain poor. But it is inconceivable that the organization and processes associated with the visit are not being improved.
label: negativo

text_clean: Esperaba más Fuimos al Circuito Mágico del Agua por nuestra cuenta, no con guía ni tour. La entrada cuesta alrededor de 10 soles, mucho más barato que pagar un tour. El recorrido es rápido y, aunque las fuentes son llamativas, no me pareció nada extraordinario. Además, hay muchos vendedores ambulantes, lo que le quita un poco de encanto al paseo. En resumen, no está mal, pero tampoco es una experiencia imperdible.

text_clean: Very interesting place. It is a man made pyramid made out of sun dried mud bricks. On a sunny day you will be able to see the ocean from the top of the pyramid. They offer guided tours with a lot of information regarding the construction, the remains found in it, the daily life of the inhabitants, their diet, and costumes. Don't forget to wear long sleeves, sunscreen, and a hat. The visit takes an hour or more depending on the number of tourists and Lima has an unforgiven sun during the summer, it is located in a desert area after all.

text_clean: The most beautiful "low" in Peru! The depth of the Colca Canyon, more than 3,000 meters (twice as deep as the Grand Canyon in the USA), is dazzling and breathtaking at the same time. Most tourists may come here for hiking, but especially to see the majestic Andean condor (Vultur gryphus) floating on the thermals. With its wingspan of over three meters, this is one of the largest birds in the world in terms of wingspan. The part between Chivay and Cabaconde is the most visited, this part is also the most accessible. The part between Cabaconde and Andamayo, the most deep and rugged part of the Colca Canyon, is not accessible so that the Andean condors can live in peace here.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Es un lindo y fascinante lugar de exposicion de una cultura que dejo grandes vestigios tanto en expuestos en sus ceramicas y entierros a sus autoridades. El punto "flaco" de lugar es que fui con mi mama de 80 años, y no vi silla de ruedas ni nadie nos ofrecio, para que se pudiera movilizar en ella. Tambien deberia haber algunos arboles o algo que de sombra en el recorrido de la entrada principal al museo, para cuando el sol este fuerte. Otro es que deberian poner mas iluminacion en los textos que estan en las muestras, ya que pasado los 40 años, nos baja la vision.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: A set of biomorphic, phytomorphic and geometric geoglyphs designed and executed on the Nazca and Palpa desert, department of Ica, Peru, are known as the “Nazca lines”. It is often believed that these lines can only be seen from the sky. For this reason, they have generated all kinds of questions and speculations. Were the Nazca lines dedicated to the gods? Was there possible technology at that time for the magnitude of this work? Were they runways for prototype aviation from the Nazca culture or, beyond, for extraterrestrial beings? Were they made by human hands? The truth is that the beauty of the Nazca lines can be seen from the top of the surrounding hills, which radically changes the way of understanding them. In fact, this is how the Peruvian archaeologist Toribio Mejía Xesspe discovered them in the 20th century, specifically in 1927. Interest in studying these geoglyphs did not spread until 1940, when Paúl Kosok and María Richie, two international researchers, published a series of aerial photographs taken by the National Aerographic Service. These geoglyphs, that is, these inscriptions on large tracts of land, are enormous in size and can be easily observed from above, which has given rise to countless theories about the purpose of their creation.

text_clean: Don’t do the Nazca Line flight, it’s expensive and not that impressive. The tour costed $79 per person and when we get to the airport we still have to pay another 77 soles(about $21)for airport tax. The flight itself is horrible especially if you have motion sickness because the plane is small and very shaky. The pilot will just tell you to look left and right to look at the lines. Make you even more dizzy. The Nazca Line, in my opinion, doesn’t look like something ancient.

text_clean: Amazing archeological site. The only drawback is that they enforce a circuit through the ruins and you cannot roam at your pleasure. Combine this with the fact that there are no toilet facilities inside the park, and you have a potential problem. If you have a physiological need after you enter the park and start the circuit, you have to quickly complete the circuit (no going back) and exit the park to use the toilets outside the park. Then you find that the entrance ticket does not allow re-entry so you have to plead with the security post to be admitted again so you can see the rest of the site, and your pleas could fall on deaf ears. I am warning you here, so you use the facilities (2 PEN charge) BEFORE you enter the site. Maybe someone from the Machupicchu management will read this and correct this poor design decision.

Then you find that the entrance ticket does not allow re-entry so you have to plead with the security post to be admitted again so you can see the rest of the site, and your pleas could fall on deaf ears. I am warning you here, so you use the facilities (2 PEN charge) BEFORE you enter the site. Maybe someone from the Machupicchu management will read this and correct this poor design decision.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo en cuanto a objetos y diseño es un 10/10. PERO llegué 430pm, 1era vez que visito el museo, cuando me vendieron las entradas, nunca me dijeron q la hora de cierre estaba próxima. Después cuando estaba dentro disfrutando del museo, alguien del museo me apuró, me dijo "en 1 hr se ve todo, ajustando 45min". Ir a un museo es una experiencia, sobre todo un museo con tantos detalles como este. No pude leer ni admirar el 50% de los objetos. Cada objeto tiene un texto bastante detallado además de ser obras muy bien trabajadas, obras q uno necesita procesar con tiempo, admirar. Soy de Lima, no sé cuando regrese, lamentablemente no tuve una buena experiencia. Sí antes de venderme la entrada me hubieran dicho "señor estamos próximos a cerrar, tome en cuenta q es probable que el tiempo no le sea suficiente" ya sabría a lo q me estaba lanzando. Sí van a ir, calculen 2 hrs para admirar todo, para procesar el arte de cada obra y no ir al museo solo por entrar y ya.

text_clean: ¡Machu Picchu es una maravilla del mundo que te deja sin aliento! 🤩 Para llegar desde Lima: 1. Vuelo a Cusco: Toma un vuelo de aproximadamente 1 hora y 15 minutos hasta la ciudad de Cusco. 2. Aconéctate con la altura: Cusco está a 3,400 metros sobre el nivel del mar, así que tómalo con calma y bebe mucha agua, el mate, dulce y hojas de coca ayudan muchísimo y en varias partes de la ciudad es totalmente gratis. 3. Bus a Ollantaytambo: Desde Cusco toma un bus que te lleve a la estación de tren Ollantaytambo aproximadamente son 2 horas de trayecto. 4. Tren a Aguas Calientes: Desde Ollantaytambo, aborda un tren que te lleva a Aguas Calientes, la ciudad cercana a Machu Picchu. El viaje dura 1hora y 30 minutos aproximadamente. 5. Camino Inca (opcional): Si quieres una experiencia más aventurera, puedes hacer la famosa caminata de 4 días por el Camino Inca. 6. Ascenso a Machu Picchu: Desde Aguas Calientes, puedes tomar un bus o subir caminando hasta la ciudadela incaica. ————————————————————————- • Impresionante arquitectura: Las ruinas incas son increíbles, con vistas impresionantes desde cada ángulo. • Naturaleza exuberante: La vegetación y las montañas que rodean el sitio son simplemente hermosas. • Experiencia inolvidable: Un viaje a Machu Picchu es una aventura única en la vida, llena de historia y magia. ¡Un lugar que tienes que visitar al menos una vez en tu vida! 💯

Aconéctate con la altura: Cusco está a 3,400 metros sobre el nivel del mar, así que tómalo con calma y bebe mucha agua, el mate, dulce y hojas de coca ayudan muchísimo y en varias partes de la ciudad es totalmente gratis. 3. Bus a Ollantaytambo: Desde Cusco toma un bus que te lleve a la estación de tren Ollantaytambo aproximadamente son 2 horas de trayecto. 4. Tren a Aguas Calientes: Desde Ollantaytambo, aborda un tren que te lleva a Aguas Calientes, la ciudad cercana a Machu Picchu. El viaje dura 1hora y 30 minutos aproximadamente. 5. Camino Inca (opcional): Si quieres una experiencia más aventurera, puedes hacer la famosa caminata de 4 días por el Camino Inca. 6. Ascenso a Machu Picchu: Desde Aguas Calientes, puedes tomar un bus o subir caminando hasta la ciudadela incaica. ————————————————————————- • Impresionante arquitectura: Las ruinas incas son increíbles, con vistas impresionantes desde cada ángulo. • Naturaleza exuberante: La vegetación y las montañas que rodean el sitio son simplemente hermosas. • Experiencia inolvidable: Un viaje a Machu Picchu es una aventura única en la vida, llena de historia y magia. ¡Un lugar que tienes que visitar al menos una vez en tu vida! 💯
label: positivo

text_clean: Desde el ingreso al parque es necesario caminar con cautela, puesto que cada 5 min es posible que un trenecito con niños abordo se cruce en tu camino. El parque perdió sentido desde que incluyeron juegos mecánicos, es irónico como es que no esta permitido el ingreso a las áreas verdes pero estas vienen siendo ocupadas por puestos de venta de alimentos y bebidas y muchos otros por juegos que encarecen la vista del parque y dañan las áreas verdes, es un tipo de contaminación visual. Otro punto a resaltar es la limpieza de los servicios higiénicos, se entiende que si se realizara un cobro por el acceso debe invertirse en su mejora y limpieza. Por último pero no menos importante, si van a cobrar una entrada que sea para el acceso de ambos parques, los espectáculos pierden sentido por volverse comercial. 0/5

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo está cerrado y los restos reales de la tumba de Sipán no se pueden visitar. Según el personal, hay problemas con el aire acondicionado y tardarán un año en volver a abrir completamente al público. Se pueden visitar tres espacios con réplicas y algunos hallazgos interesantes, pero si vienes a Lambayeque a ver al “Tutankamon peruano” te irás de aquí sin verlo. Es una lástima que el Ministerio de Cultura haya dejado un espacio como este en condiciones tan precarias y que no facilite la información con antelación. Me voy decepcionado y esperando que en el futuro se tomen en serio la protección y muestra de la rica cultura arqueológica de Lambayeque.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Together with the Rainbow Mountains and the Machu Picchu my highlight of visiting Peru. The lines look absolutely stunning from the airplane. Got to take so many great pictures of the different figures. Take a motion sickness pill before the flight as the winds are quite strong and the planes are very small. Most providers do hand out some strong smelling alcohol on a cotton pad to put in front of your nose to get you through the whole of the flight in case you do get motion sick. Once on the ground, the facility also has a room for you to lie down and recover. Great place to buy some Nazca unique souvenirs you won't find anywhere else in Peru.

text_clean: A nice place to walk around basking in the vast colonial era buildings and cathedrals. The colours of the buildings are colourfully pastel. This seems typical of this part of the world. The sun is not hot, just warm enough on most days and there are no rains. The plazas and the buildings are vast, so go girded to walk long distances and carry a bottle of water. Walk in group, don't risk it alone. See the Art Museum to understand some history of the place.

text_clean: Fuimos a la salida de un curso el día domingo, y esta fue mi segunda vez asistiendo. Sin embargo, por segunda vez consecutiva noté los mismos detalles que deberían mejorar. Los recorridos son demasiado planos y se vuelven algo monótonos, además de que los grupos son muy grandes, lo que hace que no todos los visitantes presten atención o se sientan incluidos. En mi caso, la señorita que guiaba avanzaba muy rápido y parecía dirigirse solo a un grupo reducido de personas, dejando de lado a los demás, sin atender las dudas que teníamos. También avanzaba a su propio ritmo, sin esperar que el resto del grupo se reagrupe, lo cual me pareció poco considerado. Fuera de esos puntos, el lugar en sí está muy bien cuidado. Se nota el cariño y el buen manejo que tienen, ya que todo se ve limpio, ordenado y en excelente estado. Aunque todo está muy bien conservado, hay ciertas partes como la zona final del recorrido, en la parte más alta de la huaca endonde se sienten algunos tramos un poco peligrosos, sobre todo al descender, ya que hay curvas pronunciadas sin barandas o elementos de seguridad que podrían evitar accidentes. En general, es un lugar valioso y bien preservado, pero el servicio de guiado y la seguridad en algunos tramos podrían mejorar mucho la experiencia del visitante.

En mi caso, la señorita que guiaba avanzaba muy rápido y parecía dirigirse solo a un grupo reducido de personas, dejando de lado a los demás, sin atender las dudas que teníamos. También avanzaba a su propio ritmo, sin esperar que el resto del grupo se reagrupe, lo cual me pareció poco considerado. Fuera de esos puntos, el lugar en sí está muy bien cuidado. Se nota el cariño y el buen manejo que tienen, ya que todo se ve limpio, ordenado y en excelente estado. Aunque todo está muy bien conservado, hay ciertas partes como la zona final del recorrido, en la parte más alta de la huaca endonde se sienten algunos tramos un poco peligrosos, sobre todo al descender, ya que hay curvas pronunciadas sin barandas o elementos de seguridad que podrían evitar accidentes. En general, es un lugar valioso y bien preservado, pero el servicio de guiado y la seguridad en algunos tramos podrían mejorar mucho la experiencia del visitante.
label: neutro

text_clean: Lo que debería ser un espacio monumental se ve malogrado por «innovaciones» que disminuyen su valor arquitectónico e histórico: el nombre de la ciudad en letras gigantes de triplay, copiando la huachafada de otras ciudades; jardines que no se pueden pisar pese a no tener nada especial, quitando al ciudadano del disfrute de su propia ciudad; una pileta horrorosa y «modernista» donde debiera ir la estatua del fundador de la ciudad. Lo que debería ser la mejor estampa de la que fuera durante siglos la ciudad más rica e importante del hemisferio es una Disneyland-ificación de baja calidad. Pongo la pileta, uno de los pocos artefactos que aún no mancillan.
